definition: Carry trade returns exhibit negative skewness (-0.8 to -1.5 for G10) and fat tails (excess kurtosis 3-8), with max monthly drawdowns of -8% to -15%. Standard VaR underestimates drawdown frequency and magnitude by 2-5x. The Sharpe ratio of ~0.5 has a 95% CI of [0.15, 0.85], meaning the carry premium compensates for crash risk, not alpha.
evidence: Five events exceeding 4-sigma under Gaussian assumptions in 28 years of data
Sharpe 95% CI of [0.15, 0.85] — cannot reject true Sharpe below equity premium
Max monthly drawdowns of -8% to -15% across G10 carry strategies
Brunnermeier-Nagel-Pedersen (2009) and Farhi-Gabaix (2016) confirm crash risk compensation mechanism
Five 4-sigma carry drawdowns in 28 years (1998, 2008, 2015, 2020, 2024) versus one expected per ~2,600 years
Statistics identical across all three agents' independent calculations
Standard VaR models underestimate carry drawdown frequency and magnitude by 2-5x
Distribution is fundamentally non-Gaussian, not slightly fat-tailed
Five 4σ+ events in 28 years vs Gaussian prediction of ~0.003 events
Carry-equity correlation amplifies from 0.3-0.4 to 0.7-0.8 during stress
Debate pair_3: 'the most overdetermined empirical finding in the entire analysis'
No agent contests the statistics across all debate pairs
open_questions: Has post-GFC central bank intervention structurally altered the tail distribution?
Is the carry Sharpe declining as more capital chases the strategy (out-of-sample Sharpe 0.25-0.35 for 2015-2024)?
Whether higher-moment risk metrics can be reliably estimated in real-time given regime shifts
Whether the frequency of tail events is increasing due to algorithmic trading and position concentration
Whether standard risk models can be patched or must be fundamentally replaced to accommodate this distribution
